## Assignment Overview

In this lab, you will apply the spatial predictive modeling techniques demonstrated in the class exercise using a different 311 service request type of your choice as the predictor variable to predict burglaries in Chicago. You will build a complete spatial predictive model, document your process, and interpret your results.

### Learning Objectives

By completing this assignment, you will demonstrate your ability to:

-   Adapt example code to analyze a new dataset
-   Build spatial features for predictive modeling
-   Apply count regression techniques to spatial data
-   Implement spatial cross-validation
-   Interpret and communicate model results
-   Critically evaluate model performance

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 1: Choose Your 311 Violation Type

I choose Pothole Repair Requests to use as a predictor in this assignment. My reasoning for choosing potholes as a predictor is that under-served and maintenance-neglected areas often have higher crime rates. In this lab, we will run some spatial analyses and create models to see if that is true.

### Getting the Data

To get the data, visit the Chicago 311 Service Requests dataset:

<https://data.cityofchicago.org/stories/s/311-Dataset-Changes-12-11-2018/d7nq-5g7t>

## Step 2: Analysis

#### Part 1: Data Loading & Exploration

-   Load your 311 data and Chicago spatial boundaries
-   Create visualizations showing the spatial distribution of your violation type
-   Describe patterns you observe

First, let's set up and load in our necessary r libraries, format some outputs, and create a consistent theme


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
### First let's load necessary libraries
library(tidyverse)      # Data manipulation
library(sf)             # Spatial operations
library(here)           # Relative file paths
library(viridis)        # Color scales
library(terra)          # Raster operations (replaces 'raster')
library(spdep)          # Spatial dependence
library(FNN)            # Fast nearest neighbors
library(MASS)           # Negative binomial regression
library(patchwork)      # Plot composition (replaces grid/gridExtra)
library(knitr)          # Tables
library(kableExtra)     # Table formatting
library(classInt)       # Classification intervals


# Spatstat for analyzing point patterns - split into sub-packages
library(spatstat.geom)    # Spatial geometries
library(spatstat.explore) # Spatial exploration/KDE

# Set options
options(scipen = 999)  # This gets rid of scientific notation
set.seed(5080)         # Reproducibility, could be any number

# Create consistent theme for visualizations
theme_crime <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 1),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray30", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.position = "right",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      axis.text = element_blank(),
      axis.title = element_blank()
    )
}

# Set as default
theme_set(theme_crime())

cat("✓ All packages loaded successfully!\n")
```

Now let's visualize potholes in Chicago


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Simple point map of potholes
p1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_sf(data = potholes, color = "#d62828", size = 0.1, alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Pothole Locations",
    subtitle = paste0("Chicago 2017, n = ", nrow(potholes))
  )

# Density surface map of potholes
p2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_density_2d_filled(
    data = data.frame(st_coordinates(potholes)),
    aes(X, Y),
    alpha = 0.7,
    bins = 8
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(
    option = "plasma",
    direction = -1,
    guide = "none"  # Modern ggplot2 syntax (not guide = FALSE)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Density Surface",
    subtitle = "Kernel density estimation"
  )

# Combine plots using patchwork to see them side by side
p1 + p2 + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Spatial Distribution of Potholes in Chicago",
    tag_levels = 'A'
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


In the created maps, we can see that there have been potholes reported on just about every street in the city. In the kernel density map, we can see that reported potholes are concentrated downtown and on the north side more than elsewhere. Based on knowledge of the city, one could hypothesize that the high density of potholes downtown and the north side is due to residents reporting potholes more than south and west side residents.

#### Part 2: Fishnet Grid Creation

-   Create a 500m x 500m fishnet grid
-   Aggregate your violations to grid cells
-   Visualize the count distribution


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create 500m x 500m grid
fishnet <- st_make_grid(
  chicagoBoundary,
  cellsize = 500,  # 500 meters per cell
  square = TRUE
) %>%
  st_sf() %>%
  mutate(uniqueID = row_number()) %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271') 

# Keep only cells that intersect Chicago
fishnet <- fishnet[chicagoBoundary, ]

# View basic info
cat("✓ Created fishnet grid\n")
```

#### Part 3: Spatial Features

-   Calculate k-nearest neighbor features
-   Perform Local Moran's I analysis
-   Identify hot spots and cold spots
-   Create distance-to-hotspot measures
-   Join any additional contextual data if you are looking for more to do and really get into this (e.g., demographics, land use)

Let's create our Kernel Density Estimate baseline that we will use to compare our model to later


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Convert burglaries to ppp (point pattern) format for spatstat
burglaries_ppp <- as.ppp(
  st_coordinates(burglaries),
  W = as.owin(st_bbox(chicagoBoundary))
)

# Calculate KDE with 1km bandwidth
kde_burglaries <- density.ppp(
  burglaries_ppp,
  sigma = 1000,  # 1km bandwidth
  edge = TRUE    # Edge correction
)

# Convert to terra raster (modern approach, not raster::raster)
kde_raster <- rast(kde_burglaries)

# Extract KDE values to fishnet cells
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    kde_value = terra::extract(
      kde_raster,
      vect(fishnet),
      fun = mean,
      na.rm = TRUE
    )[, 2]  # Extract just the values column
  )

cat("✓ Calculated KDE baseline\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
✓ Calculated KDE baseline
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = kde_value), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "KDE Value",
    option = "plasma"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Kernel Density Estimation Baseline",
    subtitle = "Simple spatial smoothing of burglary locations"
  ) +
  theme_crime()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/visualize-kde-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


The kernal density map of burglaries in Chicago reinforces what we learned from our earlier visualizations in that the greatest concentrations are clustered on the South side and West/NW of downtown. There are large low density areas on the deep South of the city where Illinois borders Indiana, and far Northwest near O'Hare. We will use the Kernel Density Estimate map as a baseline to compare our model to later.

Here, I did the same for potholes to see how the kernel density maps compare


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Convert potholes to ppp (point pattern) format for spatstat
potholes_ppp <- as.ppp(
  st_coordinates(potholes),
  W = as.owin(st_bbox(chicagoBoundary))
)

# Calculate KDE with 1km bandwidth
kde_potholes <- density.ppp(
  potholes_ppp,
  sigma = 1000,  # 1km bandwidth
  edge = TRUE    # Edge correction
)

# Convert to terra raster (modern approach, not raster::raster)
kde_potholes_raster <- rast(kde_potholes)

# Extract KDE values to fishnet cells
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    kde_potholes_value = terra::extract(
      kde_potholes_raster,
      vect(fishnet),
      fun = mean,
      na.rm = TRUE
    )[, 2]  # Extract just the values column
  )

cat("✓ Calculated KDE baseline\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
✓ Calculated KDE baseline
```


:::
:::


Now we've calculated kernel densities for potholes reported in Chicago. Next, let's visualize pothole KDE on a map of Chicago


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = kde_potholes_value), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "KDE Value",
    option = "plasma"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Kernel Density Estimation Baseline",
    subtitle = "Simple spatial smoothing of reported pothole locations"
  ) +
  theme_crime()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=768}
:::
:::


The KDE map of reported potholes in Chicago shows similar patterns to the count map from earlier, but does a better job at showing higher value clusters (especially on the South Side where the pattern is less obvious in the count map). The KDE map confirms the count map's visual storytelling by highlighting the high cluster of values in downtown and much of the North Side of the city.

Let's visualize potholes and burglaries with each other to better see if there is a visual correlation


::: {.cell}


Next, we'll ruan a Local Moran's I to assess spatial autocorrelation of pothole counts across cells.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
### Now let's run a Local Moran's I to assess spatial autocorrelation of pothole counts.

calculate_local_morans <- function(data, variable, k = 5) {
  
  # Create spatial weights
  coords <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(data))
  neighbors <- knn2nb(knearneigh(coords, k = k))
  weights <- nb2listw(neighbors, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
  
  # Calculate Local Moran's I
  local_moran <- localmoran(data[[variable]], weights)
  
  # Classify clusters
  mean_val <- mean(data[[variable]], na.rm = TRUE)
  
  data %>%
    mutate(
      local_i = local_moran[, 1],
      p_value = local_moran[, 5],
      is_significant = p_value < 0.05,
      
      moran_class = case_when(
        !is_significant ~ "Not Significant",
        local_i > 0 & .data[[variable]] > mean_val ~ "High-High",
        local_i > 0 & .data[[variable]] <= mean_val ~ "Low-Low",
        local_i < 0 & .data[[variable]] > mean_val ~ "High-Low",
        local_i < 0 & .data[[variable]] <= mean_val ~ "Low-High",
        TRUE ~ "Not Significant"
      )
    )
}

# Apply to abandoned cars
fishnet <- calculate_local_morans(fishnet, "countPotholes", k = 5)
```

In the Poisson regression, our results show that three of our four predictors are statistically significant (p values effectively zero), but countPotholes was found to be less significant, with a p value of 0.1902, thus its significance questionable at best.

The intercept is 1.425, which means when all predictors are zero, the predicted burglary count is e\^1.425 = 4.157 burglaries.

countPotholes has a coefficient of 0.000979097, meaning each additional reported pothole in that cell is correlated with a 0.098% increase in burglaries in that cell. Again, countPotholes' coefficient was found to be less statistically significant than the other predictors.

potholes.nn has a coefficient of -0.004297080, meaning each additional meter it takes to reach the 3 nearest potholes (on average) is correlated with a 0.43% decrease in the number of burglaries in that cell.

countLightsOut has a coefficient of 0.008370116, meaning each additional reported street light outage is correlated with a 0.84% increase in the number of burglaries in that cell.

dist_to_hotspot has a coefficient of -0.000022082, meaning each additional meter of distance away from the nearest hotspot of potholes is correlated to a 0.002% decrease in the number of burglaries in that cell. (i.e., the closer to a pothole hotspot you are, the greater number of burglaries are likely to be found). The coefficient value is very small, but is a measure per meter, so cumulatively it can have a large statistical effect.

Poisson regression only works as an effective method when dispersion is limited. Let's check our dispersion.

Interpreting binomial regression coefficients is similar as with poisson coefficients.

The intercept coefficient of 1.50753811 means that when all predictors equal zero, the predicted count of burglaries = e\^1.50753811 = 4.513

The countPotholes coefficient of -0.00209858 means that (assuming all other are unchanged) an increase in one counted pothole is correlated with a 0.21% decrease in burglaries in that cell. In the negative binomial regression, countPotholes is even less statistically significant of a predictor than in the poisson regression. Its p value of 0.1476 renders it statistically insignificant.

The potholes.nn coefficient of -0.00568201 means that an increase of 1 meter of the average distance to the three nearest reported potholes is correlated with a 0.57% decrease of burglaries in that cell.

The countLightsout coefficient of 0.01089416 means that an increase of 1 reported street light outage in a cell is correlated to a 1.09% increase of burglaries in that cell.

the dist_to_hotspot coefficient of -0.00003079 means that an increase of 1 meter of distance from the nearest hotspot of potholes is correlated with a 0.003% decrease of burglaries in the cell. The p value of dist_to_hotspot in the negative binomial regression is 0.0721, meaning its significance is questionable compared to other variables, but still far more likely to be significant than not.

##### Comparing Poisson and Negative Binomial Regression Results

Poisson AIC: 9375.5 Negative Binomial AIC: 7642.7

Negative binomial regression is more flexible as it allows for greater dispersion in the data. Our AIC results show that the negative binomial model is more effective than the poisson at fitting the data presented. Under poisson regression, standard errors are underestimated if there is great dispersion.

#### Part 5: Spatial Cross-Validation (2017)

-   Implement Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation on 2017 data
-   Calculate and report error metrics (MAE, RMSE)

Spatial Cross-Validation tests the model on unseen data by training the model on data from nearby cells and testing on an "unseen" cell.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Get unique districts
districts <- unique(fishnet_model$District)
cv_results <- tibble()

cat("Running LOGO Cross-Validation...\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Running LOGO Cross-Validation...
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
for (i in seq_along(districts)) {
  
  test_district <- districts[i]
  
  # Split data
  train_data <- fishnet_model %>% filter(District != test_district)
  test_data <- fishnet_model %>% filter(District == test_district)
  
  # Fit model on training data
  model_cv <- glm.nb(
    countBurglaries ~ countPotholes + potholes.nn + countLightsOut + 
    dist_to_hotspot,
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # Predict on test data
  test_data <- test_data %>%
    mutate(
      prediction = predict(model_cv, test_data, type = "response")
    )
  
  # Calculate metrics
  mae <- mean(abs(test_data$countBurglaries - test_data$prediction))
  rmse <- sqrt(mean((test_data$countBurglaries - test_data$prediction)^2))
  
  # Store results
  cv_results <- bind_rows(
    cv_results,
    tibble(
      fold = i,
      test_district = test_district,
      n_test = nrow(test_data),
      mae = mae,
      rmse = rmse
    )
  )
  
  cat("  Fold", i, "/", length(districts), "- District", test_district, 
      "- MAE:", round(mae, 2), "\n")
}
```

#### Part 6: Model Evaluation

-   Compare to KDE baseline

Now, we've created a model using negative binomial regression that we can use to predict burglaries in Chicago. Let's go ahead and compare it to the KDE baseline we created back in step


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Fit final model on all data
final_model <- glm.nb(
  countBurglaries ~ countPotholes + potholes.nn + countLightsOut + 
    dist_to_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model
)

# Add predictions back to fishnet
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    prediction_nb = predict(final_model, fishnet_model, type = "response")[match(uniqueID, fishnet_model$uniqueID)]
  )

# Also add KDE predictions (normalize to same scale as counts)
kde_sum <- sum(fishnet$kde_value, na.rm = TRUE)
count_sum <- sum(fishnet$countBurglaries, na.rm = TRUE)
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    prediction_kde = (kde_value / kde_sum) * count_sum
  )
```
:::


Now that we have our final model, let's compare it to our KDE baseline map from Step 2, Part 2


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# We'll make three maps: one of burglary counts in Chicago, one of the predicted counts from the negative binomial regression model, and one from the KDE baseline map predictions.
p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = countBurglaries), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Count", option = "plasma", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  labs(title = "Actual Burglaries") +
  theme_crime()

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted", option = "plasma", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  labs(title = "Model Predictions (Neg. Binomial)") +
  theme_crime()

p3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_kde), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted", option = "plasma", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  labs(title = "KDE Baseline Predictions") +
  theme_crime()

p1 + p2 + p3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Actual vs. Predicted Burglaries",
    subtitle = "Does our complex model outperform simple KDE?"
  )
```

The model's predictions capture the sharper differences of burglaries across cells, whereas the KDE baseline's map has a more vague, less precise estimation of burglary counts by cell. The KDE baseline, at least visually, does a better job at capturing general areas of high burglary counts than the model does.

To get a better idea of how the model performs compared to the KDE estimate, we'll run some calculations in R to get statistical model performance measures (MAE and RMSE) and put them into a table for easy comparison.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Calculate performance metrics
comparison <- fishnet %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  filter(!is.na(prediction_nb), !is.na(prediction_kde)) %>%
  summarize(
    model_mae = mean(abs(countBurglaries - prediction_nb)),
    model_rmse = sqrt(mean((countBurglaries - prediction_nb)^2)),
    kde_mae = mean(abs(countBurglaries - prediction_kde)),
    kde_rmse = sqrt(mean((countBurglaries - prediction_kde)^2))
  )

comparison %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  separate(metric, into = c("approach", "metric"), sep = "_") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = value) %>%
  kable(
    digits = 2,
    caption = "Model Performance Comparison"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

## Step 3: Write Your Analysis

### What we did

Throughout the exercise, I left comments and explanations for what visuals and outputs represent, and why we do each step.

In Part 1 of this analysis, we loaded Chicago 311 violation data and spatial features and visualized our data to show spatial patterns of potholes and burglaries in the city. In Part 2, we created a fishnet grid and aggregated our data to the grid cells, creating a sort of raster overlay for the city. In Part 3, we calculated spatial features such as k-nearest neighbors and distance to hot spots which we obtained by performing a Local Moran's I analysis. We also loaded in street light outages as an additional predictor. In Part 4, we ran a Poisson regression using our predictors (including the spatial features created in part 3), we then ran a Negative Binomial regression which better addressed our data's wide dispersion, and compared the two models. In Part 5, we implemented a LOGO cross validation to assess our model's performance in predicting burglary values using our Negative Binomial model. And finally in part 6, we compared our Negative Binomial model's predictive ability to our initial KDE density baseline from Part 3 and found that our model's predictive ability is worse than just using a kernel density map to predict where burglaries will occur.

### What we found

In the end, the Negative Binomial model was stronger than the Poisson model, but still under-performed compared to just using a KDE prediction. In the future, to achieve a better predictive model, I would search for more spatial or demographic data that may help to define the characteristics of each 500x500m cell we predicted on. One major issue with the 311 dataset (The potholes and light outage data) is that the rate at which people report violations may vary greatly across neighborhoods. For instance, in a neighborhood where potholes are very common, one would probably be less likely to report a small pothole on a quiet street. On the other hand, in a neighborhood where potholes are less common, one would be far more likely to report a small pothole they come across. By including other data that helps to describe characteristics of a grid cell, the bias in the 311 dataset would have far less of an effect on the overall predictive ability of the model.
